summary: This brief news post highlights two topics: Joel's latest article about social interfaces in software, and a community resource about workspace quality for developers. The main intellectual contribution is the argument that internet software has shifted from human-computer interaction to human-human interaction, making social interface design more critical than traditional usability. Joel asserts that even perfect UI cannot compensate for poor social dynamics in software. The workspace link reinforces his ongoing advocacy for quality developer work environments.
key_points: Internet software fundamentally differs from 1980s software because it mediates human-human interaction rather than human-computer interaction
Social interface design is more important than traditional usability for software that connects people
Great UI cannot save software with a poorly designed social interface
Quality workspace design remains an important factor for software development teams
money_quotes: Software in the 1980s, when usability was "invented," was all about computer-human interaction.
But the Internet brings us a new kind of software: software that's about human-human interaction.
When you're writing software that mediates between people, after you get the usability right, you have to get the social interface right.
And the social interface is more important.
The best UI in the world won't save software with an awkward social interface.
key_insight: For internet software that connects people, getting the social interface right matters more than traditional usability — no amount of UI polish can compensate for awkward social dynamics.
